behaviors.description: The wait time between retries doubles with each consecutive failure, up to a configured maximum.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: this is backoff math/limits logic and can fail inside one retry-policy function. REAL-INFRA/MOCK-HIDE: no real OS/network/subprocess is required to reproduce wrong doubling or cap handling. Rejected higher level: behavior text is explicitly about timing formula only ("doubles with each consecutive failure, up to a configured maximum"), not transport effects.
behaviors.test_description: Test the backoff calculator directly with consecutive failure counts and a max cap; assert sequence (e.g., 100, 200, 400...) stops increasing at configured maximum.

behaviors.description: The retry logic correctly identifies a rate-limit response and computes the appropriate wait time before the next attempt.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: failure can be in local classification/parsing and wait computation logic. REAL-INFRA: no real HTTP stack is necessary if response objects include status/headers/body fields used by classifier. Rejected System: behavior scope is local decisioning ("identifies a rate-limit response and computes the appropriate wait time"), not real-network behavior.
behaviors.test_description: Feed synthetic response fixtures (rate-limited vs non-rate-limited, with/without retry headers) into retry-decision logic; assert `shouldRetry` and computed delay are correct.

behaviors.description: After the wait, the client actually resends the original request and returns the successful result to the caller transparently.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: requires interaction between timer/backoff scheduler, request executor, and response propagation to caller. A single-function unit test can miss wiring bugs (request body lost, promise not resolved with retried result). REAL-INFRA: can be caught with in-process fake transport + fake clock; real network is not required. Rejected System: behavior says "resends the original request and returns the successful result" and does not require validating real socket/HTTP-client behavior.
behaviors.test_description: Wire client with fake transport that returns 429 then 200, use controlled clock to advance wait, assert second attempt uses same request payload and `call()` resolves with final success result.

behaviors.description: After the configured maximum number of retries, the client returns a typed `QuotaExhaustedError` rather than a generic error.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: typed-error outcome depends on retry loop state reaching max-retry boundary and error mapping at the client boundary. Unit-testing only constructor/type can miss orchestration bugs that throw generic upstream errors. REAL-INFRA: no real network/OS needed; deterministic stub failures are enough. Rejected System: behavior text focuses on client contract after retry count ("configured maximum number of retries"), not real HTTP stack quirks.
behaviors.test_description: Use fake transport that always returns rate-limit failure; run through configured retry limit and assert rejection is specifically `QuotaExhaustedError` with expected metadata.

behaviors.description: The full retry chain — initial call, backoff retries, quota tracking — terminates correctly and does not loop indefinitely before surfacing the error.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: this is cross-module termination safety (attempt counter + scheduler + quota tracker), so unit tests are too narrow. REAL-INFRA: infinite-loop risk can be reproduced with in-process fakes and virtual time; no real subprocess/OS primitive is required. Rejected System: behavior is about internal chain control ("full retry chain ... terminates correctly") rather than external infrastructure faults.
behaviors.test_description: Run client with always-rate-limited fake transport and fake timers; assert attempts stop at expected bound, promise settles with terminal error, and no further retries are scheduled.

behaviors.description: Requests issued concurrently from multiple async callers in the same process all draw from the same shared quota bucket.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: real failure is shared-state handoff across concurrent callers and quota manager, which requires multiple modules/tasks interacting. REAL-INFRA: explicit scope "in the same process" disqualifies need for distributed/system infra; in-process concurrency test is sufficient. Rejected System: no requirement for cross-process coordination or real network timing anomalies.
behaviors.test_description: Create one client/quota manager and fire concurrent `call()` invocations; verify total allowed sends matches one shared bucket and that depletion by one caller affects others immediately.

behaviors.description: When multiple requests are waiting to be sent, they are dispatched in the order they were originally submitted.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: ordering bugs appear at queue + scheduler + async dispatch boundary, not just within a pure helper. REAL-INFRA: FIFO correctness can be validated with in-process controlled async/fake transport; no real OS/network needed. Rejected System: behavior text is queue policy ("dispatched in the order ... submitted"), not external infrastructure behavior.
behaviors.test_description: Enqueue several calls while quota is exhausted, then release capacity; assert send order matches submission order and completion does not reorder dispatch sequence.
